action_line: webfetch("\"url\":\"https://www.biography.com/activists/muhammad-ali-malcolm-x-relationship\",\"timeout\":60")
response:
"<webfetch-citation source_id="X4I0Yf7BX3UdF1gPrdK5X" />

John Peodincuk/NY Daily News Archive via Getty Images

In late spring 1962, [Muhammad Ali](/athletes/muhammad-ali) was a brash 20-year-old boxer who'd already won two national Golden Glove titles and claimed an Olympic gold medal. Still known as Cassius Clay (he would change his name two years later), he was already working his way into contention for the sport's heavyweight championship.

As a Black man raised in the segregated South, Clay at this point in his career was publicly careful to say the "right" things and not rock the boat on issues of America's fundamental racial inequity—even as the civil rights movement was gaining steam. But privately, he was absorbing a far different message from the Nation of Islam, a Black nationalist offshoot of mainstream Islam that preached the separation of races and rejected the nonviolent ideals of the [Martin Luther King Jr.](/activists/martin-luther-king-jr)\-led [civil rig...[truncated 9544 chars]...iend

But while he attempted to bury any show of affection for his slain friend, Ali's true feelings eventually surfaced—facilitated, in part by a spiritual transformation he underwent when turning to the more inclusive Sunni Islam after the 1975 death of Muhammad.

*Muhammad Ali: His Life and Times* recalls an encounter between the boxer and Attallah Shabazz around 1978. Although they were at an event where Ali was the main attraction, he kept his old friend's daughter by his side the entire day, and they candidly discussed his feelings about her father in later meetings.

When it came time to write his 2005 memoir *Soul of a Butterfly*, Ali was more than ready to acknowledge Malcolm X as "a great thinker and an even greater friend."

"Turning my back on Malcolm was one of the mistakes that I regret most in my life," he wrote. "I wish I'd been able to tell Malcolm I was sorry, that he was right about so many things... He was a visionary — ahead of us all."

Watch Next"

action_line: webfetch("\"url\":\"https://www.npr.org/sections/codeswitch/2016/02/25/467247668/muhammad-ali-and-malcolm-x-a-broken-friendship-an-enduring-legacy\",\"timeout\":60")
response:
"<webfetch-citation source_id="w368vs2UBQJAnQhIqkKDT" />

**Muhammad Ali And Malcolm X: A Broken Friendship, An Enduring Legacy: Code Switch** **A new book, *Blood Brothers: The Fatal Friendship Between Muhammad Ali and Malcolm X*, explores how faith brought two African-American icons together and eventually tore their relationship apart.**

![](https://media.npr.org/assets/img/2022/09/22/code-switch_tile_npr-network-01_sq-d238b5cbd3882f82ea3253c4facbe1f6fc9a78a1.jpg?s=1100&c=85&f=jpeg)

NPR

#### Muhammad Ali And Malcolm X: A Broken Friendship, An Enduring Legacy

[******· 4:40******](https://ondemand.npr.org/anon.npr-mp3/npr/me/2016/02/20160225_me_muhammad_ali_and_malcolm_x_a_broken_friendship_an_enduring_legacy.mp3?d=280&e=467247668&t=progseg&seg=12&p=3&sc=siteplayer&aw_0_1st.playerid=siteplayer) 4:40

[**Transcript**](/transcripts/467247668)

![](https://media.npr.org/assets/img/2016/02/25/chapter-thirteen_ap_sq-744687c603cebef0f51f8775c305b24702136121.jpg?s=1800&c=...[truncated 6902 chars]...ed early the next year.

**Legacy Of A Friendship**

Randy Roberts says, "One of Ali's greatest regrets — and he said as much — is that he never patched things up with Malcolm, that he never told Malcolm how important he was to him." Interestingly, Ali would also eventually leave the Nation of Islam to convert to Sunni Islam, the same orthodox Muslim faith his former mentor had embraced after leaving the Nation.

Malcolm is dead. Ali's voice has been quieted by Parkinson's disease, but the legacy of their friendship is still very much with us, say the co-authors of *Blood Brothers.*

Roberts points to the fusion of politics and sports. "Once, we viewed sports as a world apart, untouched by the political and economic and racial problems of the day," Roberts says. "Well, since Muhammad Ali, we can never maintain that fiction."

And because of his association with Malcolm X, a brilliant young boxer became an ambassador to the world and an icon in and beyond black America."

action_line: webfetch("\"url\":\"https://crimereads.com/malcolm-x-muhammad-ali-cassius-clay-miami\",\"timeout\":60")
response:
"<webfetch-citation source_id="Yq7H4hCRyEXypPKo7UT7B" />

### A year before his assassination in February 1965, the Nation of Islam minister made a trip to Miami that might have changed everything.

The sound of gunfire echoed out just as the speaker had finished welcoming his audience with the traditional Muslim greeting “Assalamu alaikum” (“Peace be upon you”). Seconds later, Malcolm X lay dying on the stage of Manhattan’s Audubon Ballroom, his body filled with eight shotgun slugs and another nine bullets from.45 caliber and 9-millimeter guns.

Article continues after advertisement

The murder 60 years ago, on February 21, 1965, was a shocking final chapter in the running feud between Malcolm and the Nation of Islam, the Black separatist organization he had helped propel to national prominence before declaring his independence from it. That part of the story — the split, and the fateful palace intrigue that followed — began a year earlier in a most unlikely place: Miami, wh...[truncated 12100 chars]...abited all those years ago: Ali’s house, a modest two-bedroom, is still there; the Hampton House, now a cultural center in which Ali’s old suite, just off the pool, has been preserved for visitors; and the Miami Beach Convention Center, where Ali defeated Sonny Liston and where, afterward, Ali and Malcolm walked out into the night, their dreams glimmering in front of them like so many stars in the sky.

*–Featured image: Malcolm X and Cassius Clay, 1964; New York World-Telegram and the Sun staff photographer: Wolfson, Stanley, photographer. / Library of Congress Prints and Photographs Division. New York World-Telegram and the Sun Newspaper Photograph Collection*

action_line: webfetch("\"url\":\"https://chapter16.org/icons-and-brothers/\",\"timeout\":60")
response:
"<webfetch-citation source_id="BUuZveJVzzGpVDQaLJRVi" />

Johnny Smith talks about the fractured friendship of Muhammad Ali and Malcolm X

In *Blood Brothers*, historians Johnny Smith and Randy Roberts chronicle the friendship of two dynamic figures: Muhammad Ali and Malcolm X. Combining meticulous research with intense writing, they reveal the importance of Malcolm X in constructing the persona of Muhammad Ali. At the same time, they explain the centrality of Ali in the history of the Nation of Islam. Muhammad Ali and Malcolm X inspired both affection and fear from the American public, even as their friendship was cracking.

Johnny Smith is an assistant professor of history and an award-winning teacher at Georgia Tech. His writing has appeared in a variety of publications, including *The Wall Street Journal*, *The Washington Post*, *The New Republic*, *Slate*, *Salon*, and *The Daily Beast*. He answered questions via email from *Chapter 16*:

***Chapter 16***: Today, both Mu...[truncated 6034 chars]...Chapter 16***: Both men are global icons, their faces and names known throughout the world. Why have Muhammad Ali and Malcolm X resonated with so many people around the world?

**Smith**: Throughout their lives Muhammad Ali and Malcolm X were globetrotters, traveling to distant corners of the world, advocating black freedom at home and abroad. Ali fought fifteen times outside of the United States—nearly a quarter of his professional bouts. In the satellite era, his boxing matches became global spectacles that attracted record viewing audiences. Those fights made him one of the most visible figures in the world. Perhaps more important, during the Vietnam War he voiced opposition against racism and anti-colonialism. Like Malcolm before him, he became an outspoken critic of U.S. foreign policy. And both men embraced orthodox Islam, a religion practiced far more outside of the United States. Ultimately, Ali and Malcolm endure as global icons because we remember them as freedom fighters."

By: HISTORY.com Editors

[Black History](/topics/black-history)

## Muhammad Ali[HISTORY.com Editors](/authors/history)

Monte Fresco / Mirrorpix / Getty Images

Published: December 16, 2009Last Updated: February 27, 2025

Muhammad Ali (1942-2016) was an American former heavyweight champion boxer and one of the greatest sporting figures of the 20th century. An Olympic gold medalist and the first fighter to capture the heavyweight title three times, Ali won 56 times in his 21-year professional career. Ali’s outspokenness on issues of race, religion and politics made him a controversial figure during his career, and the heavyweight’s quips and taunts were as quick as his fists.

Born Cassius Clay Jr., Ali changed his name in 1964 after joining the Nation of Islam. Citing his religious beliefs, he refused military induction and was stripped of his heavyweight champi...[truncated 9211 chars]...informative content. All articles are regularly reviewed and updated by the HISTORY.com team. Articles with the “HISTORY.com Editors” byline have been written or edited by the HISTORY.com editors, including,, Christian Zapata, Cristiana Lombardo and Adrienne Donica.

## Fact Check

We strive for accuracy and fairness. But if you see something that doesn't look right, [click here](mailto:support@history.zendesk.com?subject=Fact%20Check%20-%20Muhammad%20Ali) to contact us! HISTORY reviews and updates its content regularly to ensure it is complete and accurate.

action_line:
repl("Writing the CryptoPad slide deck", "const slidesMd1 = `# Cassius Clay/Muhammad Ali and Malcolm X
## Friendship, faith, power, and rupture

Serious Black history research brief, May 2026

---

## Research frame

- Question: how did Malcolm X shape Cassius Clay's transformation into Muhammad Ali?
- Focus: timeline, influence, Nation of Islam split, and rupture before Malcolm's assassination.
- Method: written archival/institutional sources first, video only supplemental.

---

## Source base

Primary / archival:
- Columbia Malcolm X Project sourcebook and newspaper list
- NYPL Malcolm X papers and photographs collections
- Library of Congress Bob Gomel photograph record
- Smithsonian NMAAHC object record

Secondary:
- Blood Brothers by Randy Roberts and Johnny Smith, via NPR and Chapter 16
- Biography.com timeline article
- History.com Ali biography
- CrimeReads book-based Miami article

---

## Milestone 1: Meeting and attraction, 1962

- Sources agree Clay and Malcolm met in 1962 through Nation of Islam circles.
- Inspected sources differ on exact location: Biography says Detroit in early June; NPR says a Saviour's Day rally in Chicago.
- What is consistent: Clay was struck by Malcolm's fearless Black nationalist public voice.

---

## Milestone 2: Influence deepens, 1963

- Their friendship matured during 1963 into early 1964.
- Clay visited Nation meetings and Malcolm's Harlem mosque.
- Malcolm became mentor, older-brother figure, spiritual guide, and political model.
- Clay began echoing Malcolm's language of racial pride and non-submission.

---

## Milestone 3: Malcolm is silenced, Dec. 1963

- After the JFK assassination, Malcolm's “chickens coming home to roost” comment triggered NOI discipline.
- Columbia's Malcolm X Project frames Dec. 1963 to Apr. 1964 as “The Silencing, Muhammad Ali, and Out.”
- This put Malcolm's personal bond with Clay inside a larger NOI power struggle.

---

## Milestone 4: Miami, Jan. to Feb. 1964

- Jan. 16, 1...[truncated 3702 chars]...")
